questions.stem: A 22-year-old university student is brought to the emergency department by his friends. Over the past week he has been sleeping only 2-3 hours per night, yet feels full of energy. He is talking rapidly and loudly, with grandiose ideas that he is a talented musician who will soon sign a record deal. He spent £3000 on music equipment in one day. He has no previous psychiatric history and no substance use. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Manic episode due to bipolar I disorder
Hypomanic episode due to bipolar II disorder
questions.explanation: The patient has classic manic symptoms (grandiosity, decreased need for sleep, pressured speech, excessive spending) with marked functional impairment and psychosis-like grandiosity, lasting >1 week. Hypomania would not cause such severity or hospitalisation. Schizophrenia lacks the mood-congruent grandiosity and episodic nature. Schizoaffective requires psychotic symptoms outside mood episodes. Cyclothymia has milder fluctuations without full manic episodes.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman presents to the GP with a four-day history of feeling "on top of the world". She has been sleeping 4 hours per night but feels rested, and has been more talkative and sociable. She started several new projects at work and is more productive than usual. Her friends have noticed she seems "full of energy". She has a history of two depressive episodes in the past three years. She is not troubled by these symptoms and they do not impair her routine. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Hypomanic episode due to bipolar II disorder
Manic episode due to bipolar I disorder
questions.explanation: The symptoms meet criteria for hypomania (elevated mood, increased energy, decreased need for sleep, increased talkativeness, lasting >4 days) without significant impairment. Bipolar I would require a manic episode (with impairment). Cyclothymia involves chronic fluctuating mood swings but not discrete hypomanic episodes of this duration. Hyperthymic temperament is a lifelong trait, not an episode. No substance use history.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man is sectioned under the Mental Health Act after becoming acutely agitated. For the past week he has not slept, believing he is on a secret mission and is "untouchable". His speech is rapid and jumps between topics. He is irritable and has punched a wall. He has no medical history and no known substance misuse. Urine drug screen is negative. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Bipolar I disorder, current episode manic with psychotic features
questions.explanation: The presentation of mania (decreased need for sleep, pressured speech, grandiosity, irritability) with mood-congruent psychotic delusions (being on a secret mission) indicates bipolar I disorder with psychotic features. Schizoaffective disorder requires psychotic symptoms in the absence of a mood episode. Schizophrenia would have more disorganised thinking and negative symptoms. Substance-induced and delirium are excluded by negative drug screen and lack of confusion or medical cause.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with known bipolar I disorder currently on lithium presents with a two-week history of feeling "wired and miserable". She describes racing thoughts, irritability, depressed mood, and urges to self-harm. She has been sleeping 3 hours per night but is exhausted. Her energy is high but she feels hopeless. She has been binge drinking. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Bipolar I disorder, current episode mixed
questions.explanation: The simultaneous presence of manic (decreased need for sleep, racing thoughts, high energy) and depressive (depressed mood, hopelessness, self-harm urges) symptoms fulfills criteria for a mixed episode in bipolar I. Major depressive disorder would not have manic features. Borderline personality disorder involves chronic instability not episodic mixed states. Cyclothymia does not reach full mood episode severity. Bipolar II cannot have manic episodes (only hypomania) and this is clearly manic severity.

questions.stem: A 36-year-old man has had three episodes of major depression over the past five years, each responding to antidepressants. He has never been hospitalised. He now presents with another depressive episode. On careful history taking, he mentions that between the depressive episodes he occasionally has days when he feels "full of energy", needs less sleep, and is very productive at work, but these are brief and do not cause problems. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The presence of hypomanic episodes (brief periods of elevated energy, less need for sleep, increased productivity) in a patient with recurrent depressive episodes points to bipolar II disorder. Major depressive disorder would lack hypomanic symptoms. Cyclothymia involves chronic mood instability but not discrete depressive episodes of this severity. Dysthymia is chronic low mood. Borderline personality disorder involves unstable relationships and identity, not episodic mood shifts.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by her family. Over the past week she has been sleeping only 2 hours per night, talking rapidly, and engaging in impulsive spending. She has no prior psychiatric history. On examination, she is euphoric and has flight of ideas. Which single investigation is most appropriate to support the diagnostic workup for her presentation?
options: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the brain
questions.explanation: First-episode mania requires exclusion of substance-induced mood disorder; urine toxicology is the key test to detect stimulant use, which is a common cause of secondary mania.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man with a history of three episodes of major depression in the past five years presents to his GP. He mentions that during some periods he felt "amazing", needed little sleep, and was very productive, but these periods never lasted more than a week. He is currently euthymic. According to NICE guidelines, which screening tool should be used to assess for possible bipolar disorder?
questions.explanation: NICE recommends the MDQ as a validated screening tool for bipolar disorder in patients presenting with depression, especially when there is a history of hypomanic symptoms.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with no known psychiatric history is brought to the emergency department with a one-week history of pressured speech, decreased need for sleep, and grandiose delusions. On examination, he has a fine tremor, tachycardia (110 bpm), and weight loss. He also complains of heat intolerance. Which single investigation is most likely to identify the underlying cause of his presentation?
questions.explanation: The presence of tremor, tachycardia, weight loss, and heat intolerance suggests hyperthyroidism, which can mimic mania. Checking TSH is essential to rule out thyrotoxicosis as a secondary cause of manic symptoms.

questions.stem: According to DSM-5 criteria, which of the following is required for the diagnosis of a hypomanic episode (as opposed to a manic episode)?
options: The change in functioning is observable by others
The episode lasts at least 7 days
The mood disturbance causes marked impairment in social or occupational functioning
questions.explanation: A key feature of hypomania is that the episode is not severe enough to cause marked impairment, but the change in functioning must be observable by others (DSM-5 criterion D). Mania often includes marked impairment and may last longer.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman with a 10-year history of bipolar I disorder on lithium maintenance is referred for a second opinion because her diagnosis has been questioned after a long period of euthymia. She reports past episodes of mania that required hospitalisation. Which of the following is the most appropriate investigation to confirm the diagnosis of bipolar disorder?
options: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the brain
questions.explanation: The SCID-5 is a semi-structured diagnostic interview considered the gold standard for confirming psychiatric diagnoses, including bipolar disorder, by systematically assessing DSM-5 criteria.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man presents with a first episode of acute mania, characterised by euphoria, grandiose delusions, and reduced sleep for 5 days. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacological treatment for this acute episode?
questions.explanation: NICE CG185 recommends an antipsychotic (e.g., olanzapine) or valproate as first-line for acute mania, but antipsychotics are often preferred due to faster onset and tolerability. Lithium is a maintenance therapy, not for acute episodes. Carbamazepine and lamotrigine are second-line options. Olanzapine is therefore the best choice.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder is currently stable on lithium but reports intolerable side effects. She is planning to conceive in the next year. She wishes to switch to another mood stabiliser. Which of the following drugs is absolutely contraindicated in this patient?
questions.explanation: Valproate is contraindicated in women of childbearing age due to high teratogenicity risk (neural tube defects, developmental delay) and is associated with a higher risk than other mood stabilisers. NICE advises against its use unless effective contraception is used and specialist advice is obtained. Lithium, quetiapine, lamotrigine, and carbamazepine are not absolutely contraindicated but require monitoring.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with bipolar II disorder presents with a 2-week history of low mood, anhedonia, and fatigue. She has no psychotic features and no hypomanic symptoms currently. She is not on any mood stabilisers. What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacological treatment for this depressive episode?
questions.explanation: NICE CG185 recommends lamotrigine, quetiapine, or olanzapine as first-line for bipolar depression. Lithium is effective for mania prophylaxis but less so for acute depression. Carbamazepine and haloperidol are not first-line. Amitriptyline (tricyclic antidepressant) is avoided due to risk of manic switch. Lamotrigine is the best option among those listed.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old man had his first manic episode 8 weeks ago, which was successfully treated with olanzapine. He is now euthymic and asks about long-term maintenance therapy. He has no comorbidities. What is the most appropriate maintenance treatment?
questions.explanation: NICE recommends lithium as first-line maintenance therapy for bipolar I disorder after a manic episode, due to its efficacy in preventing both manic and depressive relapses. Olanzapine can be used but is second-line due to metabolic side effects. Valproate is an alternative but not first-line for this indication. Lamotrigine is more effective for depression prophylaxis. Maintenance therapy is indicated after a single manic episode.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by police. He is in an acute manic episode, extremely agitated, shouting, and trying to leave. He has not taken any medication. He weighs 90 kg and has no known allergies. What is the most appropriate pharmacological intervention for rapid tranquillisation?
questions.explanation: For rapid tranquillisation in acute mania with aggression, NICE recommends IM lorazepam as first-line due to its safety and rapid onset. IM haloperidol is an alternative but carries risk of extrapyramidal side effects and QTc prolongation. Oral options (olanzapine, valproate) are too slow. Chlorpromazine is rarely used due to side effects. Lorazepam is the safest and most appropriate choice.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old man is brought to the ED by his flatmate, who reports that for the past week he has been euphoric, sleeping only 3 hours per night, talking incessantly about starting a new business, and spending excessively. He has no prior psychiatric history. On examination, he is loud, with pressured speech and grandiose ideas. Observations are normal. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Refer to community mental health team
questions.explanation: NICE guidelines for first-episode mania recommend an antipsychotic (e.g., olanzapine) as first-line treatment. Lithium is also an option but is slower and more often used for maintenance. Valproate is second-line. Admission may be required but immediate treatment with medication is prioritised.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder on maintenance lithium for 2 years is stable but attends clinic asking about stopping lithium due to side effects (tremor, weight gain). She is currently euthymic. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Check serum lithium level and renal function
questions.explanation: Before any change, it is essential to assess adherence, lithium levels, and renal function to guide dose adjustment or switching. Abrupt discontinuation risks relapse. Switching without assessment is premature.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man on lithium for bipolar disorder presents to ED with vomiting, diarrhoea, tremor, ataxia, and confusion. His girlfriend says he missed his last blood test. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: In suspected lithium toxicity, the immediate step is to confirm the level with an urgent blood test. IV fluids may be started after bloods. Activated charcoal is ineffective. Haemodialysis is guided by level and symptoms.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old pregnant woman at 28 weeks gestation with bipolar I disorder currently on lamotrigine presents with a 3-day history of elevated mood, decreased sleep, and grandiose ideas. She is hypomanic with no risk to self or others. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Start a low-dose antipsychotic such as olanzapine
questions.explanation: NICE recommends an antipsychotic (e.g., olanzapine, quetiapine) as first-line for acute hypomania in pregnancy. Lithium is teratogenic and requires monitoring. Valproate is contraindicated. Lamotrigine may be continued but increasing dose is not first-line. ECT is reserved for severe cases.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man presents to ED with elated mood, grandiose delusions, and pressured speech. He has been violent towards staff and refuses medication. He is not under a section. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Arrange urgent admission under Mental Health Act
questions.explanation: The patient is violent and refuses oral medication; immediate management requires rapid tranquillisation, typically intramuscular haloperidol (with or without benzodiazepine). Oral options are not feasible. Detention under the MHA is important but the immediate priority is behavioral control.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by her mother. Over the past two weeks she has been euphoric, talking non-stop, sleeping only 2 hours per night, and planning multiple grandiose projects. She has become irritable and verbally aggressive. Which single finding would most clearly indicate that this is a severe manic episode necessitating urgent psychiatric review for possible compulsory admission?
questions.explanation: Grandiose delusions are a psychotic feature; their presence indicates a severe manic episode with high risk of harm, requiring urgent assessment for hospitalisation. The other options are core manic symptoms but not specific red flags for severity.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man with known bipolar I disorder, currently euthymic on lithium, presents with a 3-day history of low mood, anhedonia, and poor concentration. He denies suicidal ideas. Which single finding would most strongly suggest a change from a depressive episode to a mixed episode, a recognised psychiatric emergency?
questions.explanation: Increased energy in a depressed patient suggests a mixed state, which carries high suicide risk and requires urgent intervention. The other options are typical depressive symptoms without manic features.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman with known bipolar disorder is brought to the ED by police due to bizarre behaviour. She is extremely agitated, talking rapidly, and appears frightened. She has not slept for 5 days. She was started on haloperidol by her GP 3 days ago. Which single clinical sign should alert the clinician to the possibility of neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS) as a complication?
questions.explanation: Hyperthermia (temperature >38°C) is a cardinal sign of NMS and, in a manic patient recently started on antipsychotics, should prompt immediate suspicion of this life-threatening emergency. Tachycardia, diaphoresis, and rigidity are also features, but hyperthermia is the most specific red flag.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with bipolar disorder on long-term lithium treatment presents with tremor, ataxia, and dysarthria. His lithium level is 1.8 mmol/L (therapeutic range 0.6-1.2). Which single clinical finding would indicate that he has developed the most severe, life-threatening form of lithium toxicity?
questions.explanation: Seizures in the context of lithium toxicity indicate severe neurotoxicity and risk of permanent neurological damage or death, requiring urgent haemodialysis. Tremor, nystagmus, and altered mental status are earlier signs, while nausea is a minor side effect.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman, 2 weeks postpartum, presents with rapidly changing mood, irritability, and confusion. She is not sleeping and has been making unusual phone calls. Which single finding would most strongly suggest postpartum psychosis (a psychiatric emergency) rather than postpartum depression?
questions.explanation: Delusions are a psychotic symptom; their presence in the early postpartum period is characteristic of postpartum psychosis, an emergency requiring immediate assessment due to risk of harm to self or infant. The other options are non-specific and can occur in postpartum depression.
